beats.activity: Wake up, shower, coffee, and check the morning drizzle from the window.
beats.reasoning: Starting the day at home in Wedgwood; yesterday's low energy (45/100) means a slower morning. The rain outside suggests staying indoors initially—no rush to head out into the grey.

beats.activity: Drive downtown to the workshop, taking surface streets to avoid SR-520 closure and rain delays.
beats.reasoning: SR-520 eastbound is closed 9am–noon; leaving early avoids peak gridlock. Steady rain until 2pm means a wet commute, but Camila prefers to get to the workshop before the day really starts.

beats.activity: Work in the workshop on custom furniture pieces; focus on joinery and finishing in the quiet morning.
beats.reasoning: Carpenter's rhythm: early arrival allows uninterrupted work before lunch. The rain keeps the workshop cool and calm, perfect for detailed handwork. No rush to leave until noon.

beats.activity: Grab a quick lunch indoors at a nearby spot; rain is still steady, so no wandering.
beats.reasoning: Salumi worked yesterday, familiar lunch spot. Rain continues until 2pm, so indoor meal is practical. Quick turnaround—back to work by 1pm.

beats.activity: Return to workshop; afternoon deep work on a custom piece with meditative focus.
beats.reasoning: Rain clears by 2pm, but Camila is already in the flow at the workshop. Long afternoon block allows the meditative state she craves. Low energy yesterday means less social energy today—work is the priority.

beats.activity: Drive home from downtown, taking surface streets; rain has cleared, early evening light breaking through.
beats.reasoning: Heading home as the evening clears. SR-520 closure no longer relevant (ends at noon). Surface-street route is familiar and allows time to decompress.

beats.activity: Quick errand: stop at a local bookstore to browse new arrivals and pick up a poetry collection.
beats.reasoning: Camila can't resist a bookstore. University District is on the way home from downtown; evening light is now clear. Poetry feeds her creative side after a solid work day.
Short drive back to Wedgwood to settle in for the evening.

beats.activity: Cook a simple dinner, read the new poetry collection, and sit outside to watch the evening settle—clearer skies now.
beats.reasoning: Mirror yesterday's evening magic: cooking, poetry, and outdoor time as the day cools. The rain has passed; Camila can enjoy the June evening she craves.

diary: Started the day watching rain streak down the kitchen window with my coffee—the kind of grey morning that makes you want to stay in bed. Took surface streets downtown to avoid the SR-520 mess; smart call. The workshop was quiet this morning, which I needed. Something about steady rain and hand-planing wood just works. Grabbed lunch at Salumi and stayed put instead of wandering in the drizzle. Afternoon dissolved into that flow state where hours vanish—I was deep in joinery work, lost in the grain and the angles. By evening, the clouds broke and the light turned golden. Stopped by University Book Store on the way home, picked up a new poetry collection I'd been wanting. Cooked something simple, sat on the porch with the book as the sky cleared completely. Feels like the day finally exhaled.
thought_process: The SR-520 closure could've derailed me, but I was already thinking surface streets before I left—the rain made that obvious. I wanted the workshop quiet today anyway, so the weather actually worked in my favor. Staying in at lunch felt right given the steady rain; no point fighting it. The afternoon deep work came naturally because nothing interrupted the morning's momentum. I chose the bookstore detour almost impulsively—needed something tactile and different after hours of focused making. The poetry felt like the right counterweight to all that precision. The clearing skies by evening shifted my whole mood; I went from resigned-to-grey to genuinely settled.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
